task: I just got a written estimate for a repair, and now the final number is coming in higher than what I was told at first. Can you help me think through what to ask for before I agree to anything?

turns.agent_response:
Yes — before you agree, ask for these in writing:

1. **Itemized final invoice**  
   - Every labor, parts, fee, tax, and charge listed separately.

2. **Original estimate and any change orders**  
   - A copy of the estimate you first approved.
   - Any written authorization for extra work or added charges.

3. **Explanation for each increase**  
   - For every amount above the estimate, ask:  
     - what changed,  
     - when it changed, and  
     - who approved it.

4. **Contract clause or policy supporting the charge**  
   - Ask them to point to the exact term that allows:
     - higher labor,
     - extra fees,
     - parts markup,
     - diagnostic charges,
     - trip charges, or
     - disposal/shop supply fees.

5. **Proof of any rate increase**  
   - If labor or fees went up, ask for written notice of the new rate and the date it took effect.

6. **Date and time tied to each line item**  
   - Especially for labor or service calls, ask what date/time each charge applies to.

7. **Full company name and license number**  
   - If available, before you authorize anything else.

You can send something short like:

> Please send the itemized final invoice, the original estimate, any signed change orders, and written explanations for each charge that exceeds the estimate. For each increase, please identify the contract clause or policy relied on, the date/time of the service tied to the charge, and any notice of rate or fee changes. I’m holding approval until I can review this in writing.

If you want, I can turn that into a tighter dispute message with your account/reference number and a deadline.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty good, but I’d want it tighter and a little more defensive. No blurting out more than necessary.

What I’d ask for before I agree:
- the original written estimate
- the final itemized bill
- any change order or written approval for extra work
- exact reason for every amount above the estimate
- date and time the extra work was done
- name and license number of the person who approved or performed it
- the company policy or contract line that allows the increase

And I would say plainly: I do not authorize payment above the estimate until I see the paperwork.

If you want, make it into a short letter I can actually send.

turns.agent_response:
Absolutely — here’s a tighter, more defensive version you can send:

> Please send, in writing:  
> 1) the original written estimate,  
> 2) the final itemized bill,  
> 3) any change order or written approval for extra work,  
> 4) the exact reason for every amount above the estimate,  
> 5) the date and time the extra work was performed,  
> 6) the name and license number of the person who approved or performed it, and  
> 7) the contract term or company policy authorizing the increase.  
>   
> I do not authorize payment above the estimate until I review the supporting paperwork.

If you want, I can make it even firmer or turn it into a one-paragraph email.

user_simulator.text: That’ll do. Short, firm, no extra chatter. I can use that.
